components.name: nui-accordion
components.description: Expandable content sections using native <details>/<summary> with smooth animations and exclusive mode.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-accordion ## Design Philosophy Accordions and tabs solve similar problems (too much content for the available space) but with different trade-offs. While tabs show one panel at a time with navigation always visible, accordions: - Allow multiple sections open simultaneously - Show section headers even when collapsed - Work better for mobile where horizontal tab space is limited NUI's accordion uses the native `
`/`` elements as its foundation. This provides: - Built-in keyboard support (Enter/Space to toggle) - Built-in accessibility (screen readers understand the pattern) - Works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement) ## Structure ```html
First Section

Content for the first section

Second Section

Content for the second section

 ``` The `` element is always visible. Clicking it toggles the visibility of sibling content. The browser handles the toggle behavior natively. ## Progressive Enhancement Without JavaScript, the accordion works perfectly: - Each `
` can be expanded/collapsed - Multiple sections can be open - Keyboard navigation works With JavaScript, NUI adds: - Smooth height animations - Exclusive mode option (only one open at a time) - Consistent styling integration ## Exclusive Mode Add the `exclusive` attribute to allow only one section open at a time: ```html
...
...
 ``` When a section is opened, others automatically close. This mimics tab behavior while keeping the accordion visual style. ## When to Use **Use accordions when:** - Content is supplemental (users don't need to see everything) - On mobile where tabs might not fit - FAQs, where users scan headers to find relevant content **Use tabs when:** - All content is equally important - Users switch between views frequently - Content fits well in the available width

components.name: nui-app-header
components.description: Application top bar with left/center/right slot zones, sidebar toggle, and status badge support.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-app-header

> **Layout Reference:** For the full structural rules of building an application shell, refer to the `nui-app` guide in `Playground/pages/components/app-layout.html`.

## Key Rules
- This is a structural component meant to live inside `<nui-app>`. 
- Use the `slot` attribute (`slot="left"`, `slot="center"`, `slot="right"`) to place content inside the header.
- Status badges are added directly to child elements (like buttons) via `data-badge="3"` or `data-badge=""`. Do not create separate badge wrapper DOM elements.
- Toggle sidebars using `data-action="toggle-sidebar"` or `data-action="toggle-sidebar:right"`.

components.name: nui-app
components.description: Application shell creating CSS Grid layout with fixed regions: header, sidebar, content, and optional footer.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: App Layout Components (nui-app, nui-app-header, nui-sidebar, nui-content)

> **Global Note:** Always follow the global rules in `Playground/README.md`. Use the correct Light DOM wrappers.

## Critical Layout Rules (follow exactly)
- `<nui-app>` activates CSS Grid mode. It must wrap the entire application shell to create fixed regions.
- Inside `<nui-app>`, the children MUST be exactly: `<nui-app-header>`, `<nui-sidebar>`, `<nui-content>`, and optionally `<nui-app-footer>`.
- These are structural components. They MUST wrap native semantic HTML elements (`<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<footer>`).

## Strict DOM Structure (Do NOT deviate)
Do not invent tags like `<nui-top-nav>` or `<nui-side-nav>`. Do not omit the internal semantic tags.

**Correct (App Mode):**
```html
<nui-app>
	<nui-app-header>
		<header>Site Title</header>
	</nui-app-header>
	<nui-sidebar>
		<nav>Navigation links</nav>
	</nui-sidebar>
	<nui-content>
		<main>Content here</main>
	</nui-content>
</nui-app>
```

**Wrong / Will Fail:**
```html
<nui-app>
	<nui-top-nav>Site Title</nui-top-nav> <!-- Invented tag, missing <header> -->
	<nui-sidebar>Navigation links</nui-sidebar> <!-- Missing <nav> -->
	<main>Content</main> <!-- Missing <nui-content> wrapper -->
</nui-app>
```

components.name: nui-badge
components.description: Status badges and notification indicators with variants (primary, success, danger, warning, info) and data-badge overlay mode.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-badge

## Design Philosophy

Badges provide contextual status information through two complementary patterns: standalone labels for inline status indication, and overlay indicators for notification counts and alerts. The component prioritizes visual clarity while maintaining semantic meaning.

## How It Works

The badge system offers two distinct patterns:

### Standalone Badges

Use `<nui-badge>` for inline status labels like "New", "Pending", or "Active". These are semantic elements that communicate state:

```html
<nui-badge variant="success">Active</nui-badge>
<nui-badge variant="warning">Pending</nui-badge>
```

Available variants: `primary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`

### Status Indicators

The `data-badge` attribute enables notification dots and counts on any element without additional DOM overhead. CSS pseudo-elements handle the visual presentation:

```html
<!-- Notification count -->
<nui-button data-badge="3">
    <button aria-label="Messages">
        <nui-icon name="mail"></nui-icon>
    </button>
</nui-button>

<!-- Alert indicator -->
<nui-button data-badge="!">
    <button aria-label="Warnings">
        <nui-icon name="warning"></nui-icon>
    </button>
</nui-button>

<!-- Simple dot -->
<nui-button data-badge="">
    <button aria-label="New activity">
        <nui-icon name="notifications"></nui-icon>
    </button>
</nui-button>
```

## Usage Patterns

### Status Labels

For categorization and workflow states:

```html
<nui-badge variant="primary">Beta</nui-badge>
<nui-badge variant="success">Complete</nui-badge>
<nui-badge variant="danger">Failed</nui-badge>
```

### Notification Badges

For alerting users to new activity:

```html
<!-- Dynamic updates work by changing the attribute -->
<nui-button id="inbox-btn" data-badge="5">
    <button>Messages</button>
</nui-button>

<script>
// Update badge count
document.getElementById('inbox-btn').setAttribute('data-badge', '12');

// Clear badge
document.getElementById('inbox-btn').removeAttribute('data-badge');

components.name: nui-banner
components.description: Edge-anchored notification banners with auto-close, priority levels (info/alert), and singleton per placement.
components.llmGuide: # LLM Guide: nui-banner ## Design Philosophy Banners communicate status and feedback without interrupting user flow. Unlike dialogs which demand attention, banners appear at the edge of the screen (top or bottom) and can auto-dismiss. NUI provides two ways to show banners: the declarative component for static alerts, and the JavaScript API for dynamic notifications. ## Toast Notifications (JavaScript API) For transient feedback, use the factory method: ```javascript nui.components.banner.show({ content: 'Settings saved', priority: 'info', // 'info' or 'alert' placement: 'bottom', // 'top' or 'bottom' autoClose: 3000 // ms, or 0 to persist }); ``` This creates a banner element, positions it fixed to the viewport edge, and manages its lifecycle. Multiple banners stack vertically. ## Inline Banners (HTML) For persistent alerts within page content: ```html ⚠ Your subscription expires in 3 days ``` Inline banners flow in the document like any other block element. They support icons, rich content, and action buttons. ## Priority Levels Banners have two priority levels: - **info** - General information, lower visual prominence - **alert** - Important information, higher visual prominence Use "alert" sparingly; if everything is urgent, nothing is. ## Banner vs Dialog Use **banners** for: - Success/failure feedback - Background process completion - Non-blocking notifications Use **dialogs** for: - Decisions requiring user input - Critical warnings that need acknowledgment - Forms that gather information

components.name: nui-button
components.description: Styled wrapper around native buttons, including icon buttons and variant styling.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-button & nui-button-container

## Design Philosophy

The button component embodies NUI's core principle: **enhance, don't replace**. Rather than creating a synthetic button from divs with click handlers, `nui-button` wraps the native `<button>` element and adds visual styling while preserving all native behaviors.

This matters because native buttons have capabilities no div can replicate:
- Keyboard focus and activation (Space/Enter)
- Form submission and reset behavior
- Disabled state that truly prevents interaction
- Automatic accessibility announcements

## The Wrapper Pattern

`nui-button` is a Light DOM custom element that expects a native `<button>` or `<a>` as its child. The wrapper provides:
- Consistent visual styling (variants: primary, outline, ghost)
- Theme integration via CSS custom properties
- Optional icon spacing and alignment

The inner element provides:
- All native button behaviors
- Accessibility attributes (aria-label, disabled)
- Form integration (type="submit", type="reset")

This separation keeps concerns clear: the wrapper handles appearance, the inner element handles semantics and behavior.

## Button Variants

Variants change visual emphasis, not behavior:
- **Default** - Standard action
- **Primary** - Main call-to-action in a context
- **Outline** - Secondary actions, de-emphasized
- **Ghost** - Subtle actions, icon buttons

Choose variants based on visual hierarchy, not functional importance. A destructive delete action might be outline or ghost depending on how prominently it should appear.

## Button Containers

`nui-button-container` groups related buttons with consistent spacing and alignment. It uses CSS custom properties for gaps and alignment, ensuring consistency across the application.

Use containers instead of ad-hoc flexbox wrappers to maintain design system consistency.

## Icon Integration

Icons inside buttons use `nui-icon` with a text fallback. The fallback displays if the icon sprite fails to load, ensuring the button remains functional.

Place icons before text for standard buttons, or use icon-only buttons when space is constrained. Icon-only buttons should have explicit `aria-label` attributes on the inner button.

components.name: nui-card
components.description: Flexible content container with consistent surface styling (borders, shadows, hover states) and layout modes.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-card ## Design Philosophy Cards provide consistent surface styling while remaining flexible containers. Rather than enforcing internal layout constraints, the component focuses on presentation—borders, shadows, and hover states—leaving content structure to the developer. This separation of concerns allows cards to adapt to diverse use cases while maintaining visual consistency. ## How It Works The card is a structural wrapper that applies theme variables for: - **Surface styling** - Background, borders, border-radius - **Shadow depth** - Elevation through box-shadow - **Interactive states** - Hover and focus visual feedback Content inside the card uses standard HTML or NUI layout components. This approach lets cards contain any combination of images, text, forms, or actions without fighting against internal layout constraints. ## Usage Patterns ### Standard Card Vertical stacking of content: ```html

Card Title

Card description text

 ``` ### Horizontal Layout Side-by-side content arrangement: ```html

Title

Description

 ``` ### Image Overlay Text layered over a background image: ```html

Overlay Title

Description on image

 ``` ### Interactive Cards Cards that respond to clicks: ```html

Clickable Card

Opens a dialog when clicked

 Full card link

Linked Card

 ``` The `interactive` attribute adds hover effects and handles keyboard navigation automatically. ### Flip Cards Two-sided cards that flip on interaction: ```html

Front

Back

 ``` ## When to Use - Grouping related content and actions - Presenting items in a grid or list - Creating clickable dashboard widgets - Displaying media with overlaid text - Quiz cards, product cards, profile cards Cards work within `nui-layout` for grid arrangements or stand alone for specific content grouping.

components.name: nui-code
components.description: Code block with syntax highlighting for web languages (HTML, CSS, JS, TS, JSON) and example block support.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-code ## Design Philosophy This component provides lightweight syntax highlighting focused on web development languages. Rather than bundling a heavy general-purpose highlighter, it covers the essential web languages (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript, JSON) with a minimal footprint (~6.8KB gzipped). ## How It Works The component wraps `
` blocks and applies syntax highlighting via regex-based tokenization. It uses a placeholder token system to prevent nested span corruption during replacement.

For Playground documentation pages, you can also use `

components.name: nui-dialog
components.description: Modal and non-modal dialogs built on native <dialog> element with focus trapping, system dialogs (alert/confirm/prompt), and placement options.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-dialog ## Design Philosophy NUI's dialog system is built on the native HTML `` element, which provides focus trapping, Escape key handling, and accessibility features that would be error-prone to recreate. The `nui-dialog` wrapper adds NUI styling, animations, and positioning options. ## Two Usage Patterns ### Pattern 1: Programmatic System Dialogs For common modal interactions (alerts, confirms, prompts), use the JavaScript API: ```javascript // Alert - information only await nui.components.dialog.alert('Success', 'Changes saved'); // Confirm - yes/no decision const confirmed = await nui.components.dialog.confirm( 'Delete Item?', 'This cannot be undone.' ); // Prompt - simple form const values = await nui.components.dialog.prompt('Edit User', '', { fields: [ { id: 'name', label: 'Name', value: 'John' }, { id: 'email', label: 'Email', type: 'email' } ] }); ``` These return Promises that resolve when the user interacts with the dialog. The dialog element is created, shown, and cleaned up automatically. ### Pattern 2: Declarative Custom Dialogs For complex, reusable dialog content, declare the HTML: ```html

Settings

 ``` The native `` element handles: - Focus trapping while open - Escape key to close - Returning focus to the trigger on close - `method="dialog"` for form submission without page reload ## Dialog Structure A dialog has three regions: - **Header** - Title and optional subtitle - **Main** - Scrollable content area - **Footer** - Action buttons For system dialogs, these are generated automatically. For custom dialogs, you define the structure inside the native `` element. ## Positioning Dialogs can be positioned: - **center** (default) - Vertically and horizontally centered - **top** - Aligned to top with margin - **bottom** - Aligned to bottom (sheet-style on mobile) Position is an attribute on ``, not the inner `` element. ## Return Values Dialogs communicate results through the `nui-dialog-close` event: ```javascript dialog.addEventListener('nui-dialog-close', (e) => { // e.detail.returnValue contains the button value if (e.detail.returnValue === 'save') { // Handle save } }); ``` For forms with `method="dialog"`, the submit button's `value` attribute becomes the return value.

components.name: nui-dropzone
components.description: File drop overlay with named zones, automatic grid layout, and window-level drag event handling.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-dropzone ## Design Philosophy The dropzone component provides a container-scoped file drop overlay that appears automatically when files are dragged into the browser window. Rather than requiring users to target a small drop area, it creates an intuitive full-container overlay with named zones for categorizing dropped files. ## How It Works The component listens for `dragenter`, `dragleave`, and `drop` events at the window level. When files enter the browser, the overlay becomes visible via the `active` attribute, which CSS uses to toggle opacity and pointer-events. The overlay hides when the drag leaves the window (`relatedTarget === null`) or when files are dropped. Each zone child with `[data-drop]` receives an `.active` class on drag hover, providing visual feedback for the target area. ## Grid Auto-Layout The component automatically calculates an optimal grid layout based on zone count: - 1 zone → single column - 2 zones → 2 columns - 3 zones → 2 columns (2 top, 1 bottom) - 4 zones → 2×2 grid - 5 zones → 3+2 layout - 6 zones → 3×2 grid The layout uses `Math.ceil(Math.sqrt(count))` for column count, with incomplete rows spanning wider to fill space evenly. ## Usage Patterns ### Declarative HTML Wrap zones in `` with `data-drop` attributes for named drop targets: ```html
Images
Documents
``` ### Programmatic Creation Create dropzones dynamically using the component API: ```javascript nui.components.dropzone.create( [ { name: 'images', label: 'Images' }, { name: 'documents', label: 'Documents' } ], (detail, event) => { console.log(detail.zone, detail.dataTransfer.files); }, document.querySelector('#target') ); ``` ## Events The component dispatches these events: - `nui-dropzone-open` — Overlay became visible - `nui-dropzone-drop` — Files were dropped. Detail includes `{ zone, dataTransfer, originalEvent }` - `nui-dropzone-close` — Overlay hidden after drop or drag-leave ## When to Use Use the dropzone when you want users to categorize files during upload (like separating images from documents), or when you need a prominent, hard-to-miss drop target that covers a specific container area rather than the entire page.

components.name: nui-icon
components.description: SVG sprite-based icon component with text fallback, scalable sizing, and optional decorative mode.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-icon ## Design Philosophy Icons in NUI use SVG sprites rather than icon fonts. This choice provides several advantages: - Vector graphics scale cleanly at any size - Single HTTP request for all icons (sprite sheet) - CSS color inheritance works naturally - No Flash of Unstyled Content (FOUC) from font loading ## SVG Sprite System All icons are stored in a single SVG file (`material-icons-sprite.svg`). The `` component references icons by name: ```html ⚙ ``` The text content (⚙) serves as a fallback if the sprite fails to load. CSS hides this fallback once the SVG loads successfully. ## Sizing Icons inherit `font-size` from their parent, making them consistent with surrounding text: ```html

Settings ⚙

``` For explicit sizing, use CSS: ```html ⚙ ``` ## Accessibility Icons are decorative by default. When used alongside text, hide them from screen readers: ```html ``` For icon-only buttons, ensure the button itself has an `aria-label`: ```html ``` ## Theming Icons inherit color from CSS, making them easy to theme: ```css /* Icon matches text color */ nui-icon { color: var(--color-text); } /* Icon in primary button matches button text */ .primary nui-icon { color: inherit; } ```

components.name: nui-input
components.description: Form input components (input, textarea, checkbox, radio, toggle) wrapping native elements with styling and validation.
components.llmGuide: # LLM Guide: Form Input Components ## Design Philosophy Form inputs in NUI follow the same pattern as other components: wrap native HTML elements to add styling while preserving all native behaviors. This is especially important for form elements because: - Native inputs handle validation, keyboard navigation, and accessibility - Form submission relies on native input names and values - Screen readers expect standard form markup ## The Wrapper Pattern Each input component wraps its native equivalent: - `` wraps `` - `` wraps `

components.name: nui-layout
components.description: Content grid container with responsive column progression (1/2/3 columns based on viewport).
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-layout (Content Container) ## Design Philosophy Most layout systems offer unlimited flexibility—any column count, any breakpoint, any ratio. This often leads to inconsistent implementations and repeated responsive logic. `nui-layout` takes the opposite approach: **the constraint is the feature**. By encoding best practices directly into the component, it eliminates the need for developers to write the same responsive patterns repeatedly. The component enforces a natural column progression based on viewport math. ## How It Works The viewport math emerges from a simple principle: content must be consumable on the smallest viewport (320px mobile). This yields natural column counts: | Viewport | Width | Columns | Reasoning | |----------|-------|---------|-----------| | Mobile | ~320px | 1 | Base unit | | Tablet | ~640px | 2 | 2× mobile width | | Desktop | ~960px+ | 3+ | 3× mobile width | This means **1, 2, and 3 are the natural column counts**. The component encodes this pattern and handles responsive collapsing automatically. ## Layout Types ### Grid Layout (Default) Equal-height columns using CSS Grid. Specify the target column count for desktop; the component handles tablet and mobile automatically. ```html Card 1 Card 2 ``` ### Flow Layout Content flows vertically then wraps, creating a masonry-like effect: ```html Item 1 Item 2 ``` Add `sort="height"` to reorder items so tallest items appear first for better balance. ### Breakout Mode Break out of max-width constraints for full-bleed sections using the `breakout` attribute on any child of `nui-page`: ```html
Full-width content
 ``` Use `breakout` on any element that should span the full width of the container while siblings remain constrained for readability. ## Responsive Contract The component automatically clamps column counts at different breakpoints: | Viewport | columns="2" | columns="3" | columns="4" | |----------|-------------|-------------|-------------| | Desktop (≥768px) | 2 | 3 | 4 | | Tablet (480-767px) | 2 | 2 | 2 | | Mobile (<480px) | 1 | 1 | 1 | ## When to Use - **Grid**: Cards, form fields, dashboard widgets, galleries - **Flow**: Masonry layouts, tag clouds, image galleries - **Breakout**: Hero sections, featured callouts, visual separators (use `breakout` attribute) This component handles content layouts. For structural app chrome (sidebars, headers), use `nui-app` instead. ## nui-page Container The `nui-page` component is the recommended container for page content. It automatically constrains children to a readable max-width. Use the `breakout` attribute on any child to make it span full width: ```html
```

components.name: nui-link-list
components.description: Navigation list with collapsible groups, active state management, keyboard support, and data-driven API.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-link-list ## Design Philosophy Navigation lists are the backbone of application wayfinding. NUI provides both declarative HTML and data-driven JavaScript APIs to accommodate different content sources. The component transforms structured data (or structured HTML) into accessible navigation with keyboard support, active state management, and collapsible groups. ## Data-Driven Usage For dynamic navigation loaded from configuration or APIs: ```javascript const navData = [ { label: 'Main Menu', items: [ { label: 'Dashboard', href: '#dashboard', icon: 'dashboard' }, { label: 'Settings', href: '#settings', icon: 'settings' } ] }, { label: 'Documents', items: [ { label: 'Overview', href: '#docs' }, { label: 'API Reference', href: '#api' } ] } ]; const nav = nui.components.linkList.create(navData, { mode: 'fold' }); document.querySelector('nui-sidebar').appendChild(nav); ``` The `mode` option controls group behavior: - **fold** - Only one group open at a time (accordion style) - **tree** - Multiple groups can be open simultaneously ## Active State Management The component automatically highlights the link matching the current URL hash. It sets `aria-current="page"` for accessibility and applies visual styling. ```javascript // Set active link manually linkList.setActive('#dashboard'); ``` ## Declarative Usage For static navigation, write the HTML directly: ```html
Main Menu
  • Dashboard
  • Settings
 ``` The structure uses `
`/`` for groups, providing native collapsibility before JavaScript enhances it. ## Keyboard Navigation Link lists implement roving tabindex: - Tab focuses the list itself - Arrow keys navigate between items - Enter/Space activates links - Right/Left expands/collapses groups This reduces tab stops while keeping full keyboard accessibility.

components.name: nui-markdown
components.description: Lightweight Markdown to HTML converter with streaming support and NUI component integration (nui-code, nui-table).
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-markdown

## Design Philosophy

This component transforms Markdown into semantic HTML using the platform's built-in capabilities. Rather than pulling in heavy external libraries, it provides a lightweight, web-focused parser that covers the most common documentation needs while maintaining excellent performance.

## How It Works

The component accepts Markdown content via a `<script type="text/markdown">` block and renders it as HTML. This approach:

- Preserves formatting in source code
- Escapes HTML automatically for security
- Integrates with other NUI components (code blocks become `nui-code`, tables become `nui-table`)

### Streaming Support

For AI and LLM applications, the component provides block-level incremental rendering via three methods:

```javascript
const md = document.querySelector('nui-markdown');

md.beginStream();           // Initialize streaming containers
md.appendChunk(chunk);      // Append text incrementally
md.endStream();             // Finalize and seal the DOM
```

This pattern prevents UI thrashing when displaying streaming content, keeping sealed blocks stable while only the active block updates.

## Usage Patterns

### Static Content

```html
<nui-markdown>
<script type="text/markdown">
# Heading

**Bold** and *italic* text.

```javascript
console.log('Hello');
</script>

components.name: nui-overlay
components.description: Raw modal container for lightboxes, full-screen loaders, and custom modals without dialog structure constraints.
components.llmGuide: # LLM Guide: nui-overlay ## Design Philosophy While `nui-dialog` provides a structured modal with header/footer regions, `nui-overlay` is a minimal container for custom modal content. It provides the essential overlay behavior (backdrop, centering, focus management) without imposing layout constraints. Use overlay when: - Building lightboxes for images - Creating custom modal forms with unique layouts - Implementing full-screen loading states - Any content that needs modal presentation but doesn't fit the dialog structure ## Structure ```html ``` The inner `` element provides the native modal behavior. The overlay wrapper adds NUI styling and theme integration. ## Opening and Closing Declarative triggers: ```html ``` Programmatic control: ```javascript const overlay = document.getElementById('lightbox'); overlay.showModal(); // Later... overlay.close(); ``` ## Blocking Mode Add the `blocking` attribute to prevent closing by clicking the backdrop: ```html ``` In blocking mode, users must interact with content inside the overlay or click an explicit close button. ## Events Listen for overlay lifecycle: ```javascript overlay.addEventListener('nui-overlay-open', () => { // Overlay is now visible }); overlay.addEventListener('nui-overlay-close', () => { // Overlay was closed }); ``` ## Overlay vs Dialog **Use overlay for:** - Custom layouts (lightboxes, full-screen modals) - Content that doesn't fit dialog structure - Image galleries, media viewers **Use dialog for:** - Standard alerts and confirmations - Forms with standard header/content/footer - System-level notifications

components.name: nui-progress
components.description: Progress indicators with linear, circular, and indeterminate (busy) variants. Self-contained with ARIA attributes.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-progress ## Design Philosophy The progress component provides visual feedback for task completion and busy states. It offers multiple visual variants (linear, circular, indeterminate) through a single, self-contained element that manages its own internal rendering. The component automatically handles ARIA attributes for accessibility. ## How It Works The component is self-contained and generates all internal markup automatically. For linear progress, it creates the track and fill bar. For circular progress, it renders an SVG ring. The percentage label is calculated from `value` and `max` attributes and can be hidden with `hide-text`. Attribute changes are reactive — updating `value` immediately updates the visual progress without requiring JavaScript method calls. ## Usage Patterns ### Linear Progress Bar Display deterministic progress with automatic percentage calculation: ```html ``` ### Circular Progress Use `type="circular"` for compact or spinner-like indicators: ```html ``` ### Indeterminate States When progress value is unknown, use busy indicators: ```html ``` ### Custom Sizing Control size with the `size` attribute: ```html
``` ### Programmatic Updates Update progress by setting attributes: ```javascript const progress = document.querySelector('nui-progress'); // Update value progress.setAttribute('value', '75'); // Create dynamically const newProgress = nui.util.createElement('nui-progress', { attrs: { value: '0', max: '100' } }); ``` ## When to Use Use linear progress bars for file uploads, form completion, or multi-step processes where the user benefits from seeing overall progress. Use circular indicators for compact contexts like buttons or cards. Use indeterminate/busy states when duration is unknown or for loading spinners.

components.name: nui-select
components.description: Enhanced select with search, multi-select tags, option groups, async loading, and mobile bottom sheet.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-select

## Design Philosophy

Native `<select>` elements have limitations: inconsistent styling across browsers, no search capability, and awkward multi-select. `nui-select` addresses these while maintaining form compatibility and accessibility.

The key insight: the native `<select>` remains in the DOM and syncs with the custom UI. Form submissions work normally, screen readers access the standard control, but users get an enhanced experience.

## Structure

```html
<nui-select searchable>
	<select name="fruit">
		<option value="">Select a fruit...</option>
		<option value="apple">Apple</option>
		<option value="banana">Banana</option>
	</select>
</nui-select>
```

The wrapper provides the custom UI. The inner `<select>` provides:
- Form submission values
- Accessibility tree entry
- Native validation

## Searchable Selects

Add the `searchable` attribute to enable filtering:

```html
<nui-select searchable>
	<select>...</select>
</nui-select>
```

The search input appears in the dropdown. Typing filters options in real-time. This is especially useful for long lists (countries, categories).

## Multi-Select

Add the `multiple` attribute to the native `<select>`:

```html
<nui-select searchable>
	<select name="fruits" multiple>
		<option value="apple" selected>Apple</option>
		<option value="banana">Banana</option>
	</select>
</nui-select>
```

Selected items appear as removable tags above the input. The native select maintains all selected values for form submission.

## Dynamic Content

Update options programmatically:

```javascript
const select = document.querySelector('nui-select');
select.setItems([
	{ value: 'a', label: 'Option A' },
	{ group: 'Group 1', options: [
		{ value: 'b', label: 'Option B' }
	]}
]);
```

This replaces the inner `<select>` options and updates the custom UI.

## Mobile Adaptation

On mobile devices, `nui-select` uses a bottom sheet interface instead of a dropdown. This provides better touch targets and follows platform conventions. The same markup works everywhere - the component adapts automatically.

components.name: nui-skip-links
components.description: WCAG 2.1 Level A skip navigation links. Auto-detects landmarks or accepts custom anchors.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-skip-links ## Design Philosophy Skip links embody the principle that accessibility features should benefit all users while being essential for some. Keyboard users rely on skip links to bypass repetitive navigation and reach main content quickly. This component implements the WCAG 2.1 Level A requirement for bypass blocks. ## How It Works The component provides two usage modes: 1. **Automatic mode** - Detects `
` elements and generates appropriate skip links 2. **Declarative mode** - Accepts custom anchor elements for specific navigation needs Skip links are visually hidden until focused, appearing as the first focusable element when users press Tab. The component automatically manages `tabindex="-1"` on target elements to ensure proper focus handling. ## Usage Patterns ### Automatic Generation When placed without children, the component scans for common landmarks: ```html ...
...
 ``` ### Custom Skip Links Define specific navigation paths with anchor elements: ```html Skip to search Skip to main content Skip to footer ``` ## Integration Points - Adds `role="navigation"` and `aria-label="Skip links"` automatically - Uses `a11y.announce()` for screen reader feedback on activation - Handles focus management without scrolling animations ## When to Use - All applications with navigation before main content - Pages with complex sidebar navigation - Multi-step forms where users may want to skip instructions - Any page requiring WCAG 2.1 Level A compliance ## Testing To verify skip links work correctly: 1. Load the page 2. Press Tab - the first skip link should appear at the top-left 3. Press Enter - focus should move to the target section 4. Continue tabbing - navigation should proceed from the target element

components.name: nui-slider
components.description: Enhanced range input wrapping native <input type='range'> with custom visual styling and drag interaction.

components.name: nui-sortable
components.description: Drag-and-drop reordering with FLIP animations, touch/mouse/keyboard support, and nui-sortable-item children.
components.llmGuide: # LLM Guide: nui-sortable & nui-sortable-item ## Design Philosophy The sortable component enables users to reorder items through an intuitive drag-and-drop interface. It uses FLIP (First, Last, Invert, Play) animation technique for smooth, performant visual transitions that feel natural and responsive across all devices. ## How It Works The component handles the complexity of drag interactions including touch, mouse, and keyboard navigation. Each `` represents a draggable element within a `` container. The component tracks positions, calculates movements, and applies FLIP animations to create the illusion of elements sliding into their new positions. Drag handles (using `nui-icon name="drag_indicator"`) provide a clear affordance for initiating drag operations. The `data-id` attribute on items ensures stable identity during reordering. ## Usage Patterns ### Basic Sortable List Create a sortable list with draggable items: ```html Task content ``` ### Horizontal Layout Use `data-layout="horizontal"` for row-based ordering: ```html Item 1 Item 2 ``` ### Grid Layout Use `data-layout="grid"` for two-dimensional reordering: ```html 1 2 ``` ### Programmatic Control The component exposes methods for dynamic manipulation: ```javascript const sortable = document.getElementById('my-list'); // Get current items const items = sortable.getItems(); // [{ id, element }, ...] // Add new items sortable.addItem(`...`); // Replace all items sortable.setItems([htmlString1, htmlString2]); // Clear all items sortable.clear(); ``` ## Events Listen for the `nui-sortable-change` event to respond to reordering: ```javascript sortable.addEventListener('nui-sortable-change', (e) => { console.log('New order:', e.detail.order); // ['task-2', 'task-1', ...] }); ``` ## When to Use Use sortable when users need to prioritize, organize, or sequence items. Common scenarios include task prioritization, image galleries, playlist ordering, or any interface where item sequence carries meaning. The grid layout works well for dashboard widgets or photo arrangements.

components.name: nui-table
components.description: Responsive table wrapper with mobile card transformation using data-label attributes.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-table ## Design Philosophy Tables present a challenge on mobile: wide tabular data doesn't fit narrow screens. NUI's table component solves this by transforming the display based on viewport size while preserving the semantic table markup for accessibility. ## Structure `` wraps a standard HTML table: ```html
Name Role
Alice Developer
``` The wrapper provides: - Consistent styling (borders, spacing, typography) - Responsive transformation on mobile - Theme integration via CSS variables ## Responsive Behavior On narrow screens, the table transforms into a card-like layout: - Each row becomes a card - Column headers become labels via `data-label` attributes - Horizontal scrolling is avoided This transformation uses CSS and preserves all table semantics for screen readers. ## Data Tables For data-heavy tables, consider using the addon component `nui-list` which provides: - Virtual scrolling for large datasets - Built-in sorting and filtering - Customizable row rendering Use `` for: - Small to medium datasets (under 100 rows) - Static content tables - Simple data presentation Use `nui-list` for: - Large datasets (1000+ rows) - Dynamic data requiring sort/filter - Custom row layouts

components.name: nui-tabs
components.description: Tabbed interface following ARIA Tab Pattern with height animation and progressive enhancement.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-tabs

## Design Philosophy

Tabs organize content into mutually exclusive views while keeping all content in the DOM. Unlike navigation that loads new pages, tabs switch between content that's already present.

The NUI tabs component follows the ARIA Tab Pattern closely, using semantic HTML as the foundation:
- `<nav>` for the tab list (role="tablist")
- `<button>` elements for tabs (role="tab")
- `<section>` elements for panels (role="tabpanel")

This structure works as plain HTML (all panels visible) and upgrades to interactive tabs when JavaScript loads.

## Structure

```html
<nui-tabs>
	<nav>
		<button data-tab="profile">Profile</button>
		<button data-tab="settings">Settings</button>
	</nav>
	<section id="profile">...</section>
	<section id="settings" hidden>...</section>
</nui-tabs>
```

The `data-tab` attribute on buttons links to panel IDs. When a tab is activated:
1. The button gets `aria-selected="true"`
2. Its corresponding panel loses the `hidden` attribute
3. All other panels get `hidden`

## Progressive Enhancement

Without JavaScript, all panels are visible. The `hidden` attribute on inactive panels ensures content is accessible even before upgrade.

After upgrade:
- Only the first panel is shown by default
- Keyboard navigation works (arrows, Home, End)
- ARIA attributes are managed automatically

## Height Animation

Tab panels automatically animate height when switching between tabs of different content heights. This provides visual continuity without layout shifts.

The animation respects `prefers-reduced-motion` automatically.

## Programmatic Control

Select tabs programmatically:

```javascript
tabs.select('settings');
```

Listen for changes:

```javascript
tabs.addEventListener('nui-tab-change', (e) => {
	console.log('Active tab:', e.detail.tab);
});
```

## Use Cases

Tabs work well for:
- Settings organized by category
- Content that doesn't need URL-addressable states
- Space-constrained layouts where showing all content at once would be overwhelming

Avoid tabs for:
- Content that users might want to link to directly (use pages)
- Sequential workflows (use step indicators)
- Large amounts of content per panel (consider pages or accordions)

components.name: nui-tag-input
components.description: Tag/chip input with keyboard navigation, hidden form inputs for submission, and editable or display-only modes.

components.name: nui-tooltip
components.description: Contextual help tooltips using native Popover API with smart positioning and ARIA integration.
components.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-tooltip ## Design Philosophy The tooltip component provides contextual help and explanations without cluttering the interface. It follows the principle of progressive disclosure—showing information only when the user shows interest (hover or focus). ## How It Works The component uses the native Popover API for top-layer rendering, eliminating z-index conflicts and complex positioning logic. It automatically handles: - **Smart positioning** - Detects viewport edges and adjusts placement - **Focus management** - Works with keyboard navigation - **ARIA attributes** - Links tooltip content to target element via `aria-describedby` ## Usage Patterns ### Adjacent Placement (Default) Place the tooltip immediately after its target element. The component automatically associates them: ```html Helpful explanation here ``` ### Explicit Targeting Use the `for` attribute when the tooltip cannot be placed adjacent to the target: ```html Tooltip text ``` ### Rich Content Tooltips support formatted content including headings, paragraphs, and interactive elements: ```html Feature Name

Detailed description here

 ``` ## Configuration Options - `position="top|bottom|left|right"` - Manual placement (defaults to auto) - `offset="16"` - Adjust distance from target in pixels ## When to Use - Explaining icon buttons without text labels - Providing additional context for form fields - Showing keyboard shortcuts - Offering clarifications for technical terms ## Integration Notes The component handles all event listeners internally. For rich content tooltips, the hover area extends to the tooltip itself, preventing premature closing when the user moves their mouse toward the content.

addons.name: nui.appWindow
addons.description: Programmatic window chrome (title bar, content, status bar) for Electron frameless windows. Factory function, not custom element.

addons.name: nui-code-editor
addons.description: Editable code input with real-time syntax highlighting, caret preservation, auto-indent, and optional line numbers.
addons.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-code-editor (Experimental)

## Design Philosophy

This component provides an editable code input with real-time syntax highlighting. Unlike heavy editors like Monaco or CodeMirror, it uses a lightweight `contenteditable` approach with custom caret restoration, prioritizing fast load times and simplicity over advanced IDE features.

## How It Works

The component manages a `contenteditable` code block with:

- **Syntax highlighting** - Real-time tokenization using the same engine as `nui-code`
- **Caret preservation** - Cursor position maintained during re-highlighting
- **Smart editing** - Auto-indentation, bracket matching, tab handling
- **Line numbers** - Optional gutter showing line numbers

The highlighting approach differs from static `nui-code`: it carefully manages the editable state to apply colors without disrupting the editing experience.

## Usage Patterns

### Basic Editor

```html
<nui-code-editor data-lang="js" data-line-numbers="true">
function example() {
    console.log('Hello');
}
</nui-code-editor>
```

### Listening for Changes

```javascript
const editor = document.querySelector('nui-code-editor');

editor.addEventListener('nui-change', (e) => {
    console.log('Code changed:', e.detail.value);
    console.log('Language:', e.detail.lang);
});
```

### Getting and Setting Values

```javascript
const editor = document.querySelector('nui-code-editor');

// Get current code
const code = editor.value;

// Set new code
editor.value = 'const x = 42;';
```

## Editor Features

- **Auto-indent** - New lines inherit previous indentation
- **Bracket matching** - Auto-closes brackets and quotes
- **Tab handling** - Inserts spaces instead of changing focus
- **Paste handling** - Intelligent paste for large code blocks

## When to Use

- Configuration file editing
- Code snippet input
- Simple scripting interfaces
- Educational coding environments
- Prototypes where IDE features aren't needed

For full IDE capabilities (intellisense, debugging, multi-file), consider Monaco Editor or similar specialized solutions.

addons.name: nui-context-menu
addons.description: Programmatic floating popup menus with positioning, submenus, and keyboard navigation. Factory-based API, no custom element.

addons.name: nui-lightbox
addons.description: Image gallery with thumbnail/full-res lazy loading, keyboard navigation, carousel loop mode, and touch gestures.
addons.llmGuide: # LLM Guide: nui-lightbox ## Design Philosophy The lightbox component provides focused image viewing without leaving the page. It emphasizes performance through lazy-loading of full-resolution images and accessibility through keyboard navigation and ARIA attributes. ## How It Works The component offers two usage modes: 1. **Declarative** - Wrap images in `` for automatic grouping 2. **Programmatic** - Call `nui.components.lightbox.show()` with image arrays Key features: - **Thumbnail/full-res separation** - Display thumbnails while loading full images on demand - **Keyboard navigation** - Arrow keys, Escape to close - **Carousel support** - Optional `loop` attribute for continuous navigation - **Touch friendly** - Swipe gestures on mobile ## Usage Patterns ### Image Gallery Group images by wrapping them in a lightbox container: ```html ``` The `data-lightbox-src` attribute specifies the full-resolution image to display. ### Programmatic Lightbox Open a lightbox from JavaScript without declarative markup: ```javascript nui.components.lightbox.show([ { src: 'image1.jpg', title: 'First Image' }, { src: 'image2.jpg', title: 'Second Image' } ], 0); // Start at index 0 ``` ### Element Method For programmatic control on a specific lightbox instance: ```javascript const lightbox = document.querySelector('nui-lightbox'); lightbox.open([], 2); // Open at image index 2 ``` ## When to Use - Photo galleries and portfolios - Product image viewers - Documentation screenshots - Anywhere users need to examine images in detail The lightbox handles focus management automatically, ensuring keyboard users can navigate and close the viewer easily.

addons.name: nui-list
addons.description: Virtualized scroller with integrated search, sort, filter. Renders only visible items for large datasets.
addons.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-list ## Component Architecture `nui-list` is a virtualized scroller with integrated data operations (search, sort, filter). It renders only visible items (~20-30) regardless of dataset size. ### Internal Structure The component constructs this DOM structure: ``` nui-list (position: absolute, inset: 0) ├── .nui-list-header // Optional: search, sort dropdowns, filters ├── .nui-list-body │ ├── .nui-list-fixed // Overlay for large datasets (fixed mode) │ └── .nui-list-viewport (overflow-y: auto) │ └── .nui-list-container (sized to data.length * itemHeight) └── .nui-list-footer // Optional: action buttons, selection count ``` ### Layout Model The list uses absolute positioning to fill its container. This requires: 1. **Positioned ancestor**: Container with `position: relative` (or non-static) 2. **Definite height**: Container height must be computable (not `auto`) Without these, measurements fail: - No positioned ancestor → positions relative to viewport - No explicit height → `offsetHeight === 0` → calculates 0 visible items ### Virtual Scrolling Algorithm ```javascript const viewportHeight = list.viewport.offsetHeight; // Must be > 0 const itemHeight = measuredFromFirstItem; // All items same height const visibleCount = Math.ceil(viewportHeight / itemHeight); const startIndex = Math.floor(scrollTop / itemHeight); // Render indices startIndex to startIndex + visibleCount ``` ### Data Flow 1. `loadData(options)` receives configuration 2. Data is cloned into internal array with `oidx` (original index) 3. Filters/sort are applied → `filtered` array 4. Container height set to `filtered.length * itemHeight` 5. Scroll observer triggers render of visible window 6. `render(item)` function called for each visible data item 7. DOM elements created, positioned absolutely at `index * itemHeight` ### API Reference **loadData(options)** ```javascript list.loadData({ data: Array, // Required: array of data objects render: Function, // Required: (item) => HTMLElement search: [{ prop: 'name' }], // Optional: properties to search sort: [{ label: 'Name', prop: 'name', numeric: false }], // Optional filters: [{ prop: 'status', label: 'Status', options: [...] }], // Optional footer: { buttons_left: [], buttons_right: [] }, // Optional selection: 'multi'|'single', // Optional: default 'single' logmode: Boolean, // Optional: auto-scroll to bottom events: (event) => {} // Optional: callback for all events }); ``` **Methods** - `update(force)` - Re-render visible items - `updateData(data, skipFilter)` - Replace data array - `appendData()` - Add new items (for log mode) - `getSelection(full)` - Get selected indices or full objects - `setSelection(indexArray)` - Set selection by original indices - `scrollToIndex(index)` - Scroll to filtered list index - `cleanUp()` - Remove listeners and observers **Events (passed to events callback)** - `{ type: 'selection', value: count }` - Selection changed - `{ type: 'sort', index, direction }` - Sort changed - `{ type: 'filter', prop, value }` - Filter changed - `{ type: 'search_input', value }` - Search text changed - `{ type: 'visibility', value: boolean }` - List entered/left viewport ### Usage Patterns **Basic with template (declarative)** ```html
``` **Custom render function (programmatic)** ```javascript list.loadData({ data: items, render: (item) => { const el = document.createElement('div'); el.className = 'item'; el.textContent = item.name; return el; } }); ``` **Lazy loading (defer heavy content)** ```javascript render: (item) => { const el = document.createElement('div'); el.innerHTML = '
'; el.update = () => { // Called when element enters viewport el.querySelector('.placeholder').innerHTML = ``; }; return el; } ``` **Full-featured with all options** ```javascript list.loadData({ data: products, render: renderProduct, search: [{ prop: 'name' }, { prop: 'sku' }], sort: [ { label: 'Name', prop: 'name' }, { label: 'Price', prop: 'price', numeric: true } ], filters: [ { prop: 'category', label: 'Category', options: categoryOptions }, { prop: 'status', label: 'Status', options: statusOptions } ], footer: { buttons_left: [ { label: 'Delete', type: 'danger', fnc: handleDelete } ], buttons_right: [ { label: 'Export', type: 'primary', fnc: handleExport } ] }, selection: 'multi', events: handleEvent }); ``` ### Critical Requirements 1. **Container**: `position: relative` + explicit height 2. **Items**: Uniform height (measured from first item) 3. **Timing**: Call `loadData()` after container is visible 4. **Dialog**: Use `content-scroll="false"` on `nui-dialog`

addons.name: nui-media-player
addons.description: Custom-skinnable video/audio player wrapper over native media elements with pause-others and poster support.
addons.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-media-player (Experimental)

## Design Philosophy

This component wraps native `<video>` and `<audio>` elements with a customizable, skinnable UI. Rather than replacing the browser's media capabilities, it enhances them with consistent controls while preserving all native functionality like streaming, subtitles, and format support.

## How It Works

The component builds a custom control interface over native media elements:

- **Custom controls** - Play/pause, progress, volume, fullscreen
- **Native backend** - All media handling uses browser-native APIs
- **Auto-pause** - Optional `pause-others` prevents multiple players playing simultaneously
- **Poster support** - Video thumbnails via the native `poster` attribute

## Usage Patterns

### Video Player

```html
<nui-media-player pause-others>
    <video src="video.mp4" poster="poster.jpg"></video>
</nui-media-player>
```

### Audio Player

```html
<nui-media-player pause-others>
    <audio src="audio.mp3"></audio>
</nui-media-player>
```

### Programmatic Creation

```javascript
nui.components.mediaPlayer.create('#container', {
    url: 'https://example.com/video.mp4',
    type: 'video', // or 'audio'
    poster: 'https://example.com/poster.jpg',
    pauseOthers: true,
    attributes: {
        loop: true,
        crossorigin: 'anonymous'
    }
});
```

## Native Events

Since the component wraps native media elements, all standard media events work:

```javascript
const player = document.querySelector('nui-media-player');
const video = player.querySelector('video');

video.addEventListener('ended', () => {
    console.log('Playback finished');
});

video.addEventListener('timeupdate', () => {
    console.log('Current time:', video.currentTime);
});
```

## When to Use

- Video tutorials or documentation
- Audio playback for music or podcasts
- Media galleries where consistent controls matter
- Applications needing coordinated media playback (via `pause-others`)

As an experimental component, the API may evolve based on usage feedback.

addons.name: nui-menu
addons.description: Application-style menubar with dropdowns, nested submenus, and full keyboard navigation.
addons.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: nui-menu ## Design Philosophy This component implements the W3C ARIA menubar pattern, providing application-style menu navigation with dropdowns and nested submenus. It prioritizes keyboard accessibility and predictable behavior over visual complexity, following VS Code's interaction model for familiarity. ## How It Works The component creates a menubar where each top-level button opens a dropdown menu. Key architectural decisions: - **Element reuse** - Dropdown containers are created once and repositioned, not recreated - **State machine** - Clear states (idle, open, hovering) prevent common menu bugs - **Roving tabindex** - Keyboard navigation follows the ARIA pattern exactly - **ARIA compliance** - Full support for menubar, menu, and menuitem roles ## Usage Patterns ### Declarative Menus Define menus with semantic HTML: ```html
``` ### Programmatic Menus Create menus dynamically with the same data structure as `nui-link-list`: ```javascript const menuData = { items: [ { label: 'File', items: [ { label: 'New File', action: 'new-file', shortcut: 'Ctrl+N' }, { label: 'Open...', action: 'open', shortcut: 'Ctrl+O' }, { type: 'separator' }, { label: 'Exit', action: 'exit' } ] }, { label: 'Edit', items: [ { label: 'Cut', action: 'cut', shortcut: 'Ctrl+X' }, { label: 'Copy', action: 'copy', shortcut: 'Ctrl+C' } ] } ] }; const menu = document.createElement('nui-menu'); menu.loadData(menuData); document.body.appendChild(menu); ``` ### Nested Submenus Define hierarchical menus by nesting items: ```javascript { label: 'File', items: [ { label: 'New...', items: [ { label: 'Text File', action: 'new-text' }, { label: 'HTML File', action: 'new-html' } ] }, { label: 'Open...', action: 'open' } ] } ``` ## Keyboard Navigation | Key | Action | |-----|--------| | Left/Right | Navigate between menubar items | | Down | Open menu and focus first item | | Up | Open menu and focus last item | | Enter/Space | Activate item or open submenu | | Escape | Close menu and return to menubar | | Home/End | Jump to first/last item | ## When to Use - Desktop-style applications with menu bars - Complex toolbars requiring grouped actions - Interfaces needing keyboard-efficient navigation - Applications where users expect traditional menu patterns

addons.name: nui-rich-text
addons.description: Lightweight WYSIWYG editor using contenteditable with toolbar for basic formatting, links, tables, and images.

reference.name: accessibility
reference.description: DOM-first accessibility philosophy: semantic HTML foundation, ARIA auto-enhancement, roving tabindex, focus management.
reference.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: NUI Accessibility Architecture

## The Philosophy: DOM-First A11y

NUI's accessibility approach is built on a simple truth: **the browser knows more about accessibility than any JavaScript framework.** Instead of complex abstraction layers, NUI uses semantic HTML as the foundation and enhances it where necessary.

This means:
- Native `<button>` elements are the interactive targets (not divs)
- Native `<dialog>` provides focus trapping and Escape handling
- Native `<input>` with proper labels for form controls
- ARIA attributes are added automatically during component upgrade

The result: accessibility works even before JavaScript loads, and screen readers see standard, predictable markup.

## Intelligent Enhancement

NUI doesn't just preserve native accessibility - it enhances it intelligently:

**Icon-Only Buttons:**
When NUI sees `<button><nui-icon name="settings"></nui-icon></button>`, it:
1. Generates an appropriate `aria-label` based on the icon name
2. Hides the icon from screen readers (`aria-hidden="true"`)
3. Logs a warning so developers know it happened

**Composite Components:**
Components like tabs and menus implement roving tabindex automatically:
- Only the active item is in the tab sequence
- Arrow keys navigate between items
- Screen readers announce state changes

**Focus Management:**
Dialogs and overlays handle focus automatically:
- Focus moves into the modal when opened
- Focus is trapped within the modal while open
- Focus returns to the trigger when closed

## The A11y Utility

NUI provides `nui.a11y.announce()` for dynamic content:

```javascript
// Screen reader will announce this
nui.a11y.announce('File uploaded successfully');
```

This uses a visually hidden live region that screen readers monitor. It's the standard pattern for announcing changes that aren't focus-driven.

## Component-Specific Patterns

**nui-tabs:**
Follows the ARIA Tab Pattern exactly:
- `role="tablist"` on the navigation container
- `role="tab"` on each button with `aria-selected`
- `role="tabpanel"` on content sections with `aria-labelledby`

**nui-select:**
Adapts to the platform:
- Desktop: Combobox pattern with typeahead search
- Mobile: Native bottom sheet for touch optimization

**nui-sortable:**
Implements keyboard reordering:
- Space/Enter to "grab" an item
- Arrow keys to move it
- Space/Enter to "drop" it
- Live region announcements for each move

**nui-table:**
Responsive transformation:
- Desktop: Standard table with headers
- Mobile: Card layout with `data-label` attributes preserving column context for screen readers

## Reduced Motion Support

NUI respects `prefers-reduced-motion` automatically:
- CSS transitions respect the media query
- JavaScript animations check the preference
- No action required from developers

## Why This Matters for LLMs

When generating NUI code, understanding this architecture means:
- Don't add redundant ARIA attributes (components handle it)
- Do use semantic HTML (the foundation of the system)
- Don't use divs for buttons (breaks the enhancement model)
- Do trust the component's built-in behaviors

The accessibility is batteries-included, not bolted-on.

reference.name: api-structure
reference.description: Three-tier API namespace design: root (lifecycle), components (factory APIs), utilities (helpers).
reference.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: NUI API Organization

## The Three-Tier Architecture

NUI exports a single `nui` object organized into three conceptual layers:

```
nui.*                 → Root: Core library, lifecycle, configuration
nui.components.*      → Components: Factory APIs for data-driven UI
nui.util.*            → Utilities: Helper functions
```

This structure provides clear extension points and makes the API discoverable.

## Root Level: Core Library

**Purpose:** Library initialization, configuration, and fundamental operations.

**Key members:**
- `nui.init()` - Initialize the library (auto-called on load)
- `nui.configure()` - Update configuration
- `nui.setupRouter()` - Setup SPA router (replaces enableContentLoading)
- `nui.registerFeature()` - Register JS-only page types
- `nui.registerType()` - Register content handlers

**Design principle:** Root functions affect the entire application. They're called once at setup, not repeatedly during rendering.

## Components Namespace: Data-Driven UI

**Purpose:** Factory APIs for components that are created dynamically rather than declared in HTML.

**Two component patterns:**

**Ephemeral components** (created, shown, destroyed):
```javascript
// Dialog appears, user interacts, it closes
await nui.components.dialog.confirm('Delete?', 'Cannot undo');
```

**Persistent components** (created, appended, remain):
```javascript
// Create once, use indefinitely
const nav = nui.components.linkList.create(data);
document.body.appendChild(nav);
```

**Built-in components:**
- `dialog` - Modal system dialogs (alert, confirm, prompt)
- `banner` - Toast notifications
- `linkList` - Navigation tree from data

**Addon pattern:**
Components register themselves under `nui.components.*`:
```javascript
// In nui-gallery.js
nui.components.gallery = {
	create(images, options) { ... }
};
```

This enables a standard import-and-use pattern:
```javascript
import './NUI/lib/modules/nui-gallery.js';
const gallery = nui.components.gallery.create(images);
```

## Utilities Namespace: Helper Functions

**Purpose:** Stateless operations that don't render UI.

**Categories:**
- **DOM:** `createElement`, `createSvgElement`
- **Data:** `sortByKey`, `filter`
- **Storage:** `storage` (cookies/localStorage)
- **Interaction:** `enableDrag`
- **Environment:** `detectEnv`

**Usage pattern:**
```javascript
// Destructure for convenience
const { createElement, storage } = nui.util;

// Use directly
const el = createElement('div', { class: 'card' });
storage.set({ name: 'pref', value: 'dark' });
```

## Why This Organization Matters

**Discoverability:**
```javascript
// See what's available
console.log(Object.keys(nui.components));
// ['dialog', 'banner', 'linkList', 'icon']
```

**Extensibility:**
Addons extend the namespaces without conflicting:
- New component? Add to `nui.components.*`
- New utility? Add to `nui.util.*`
- New root function? Add to `nui.*`

**Tree-shaking:**
The namespace structure helps bundlers understand the API surface for dead code elimination.

## The Component Lifecycle

Understanding when things happen:

1. **Import time:** Module loads, code executes
2. **DOM ready:** `nui.init()` called (auto or manual)
3. **Custom elements defined:** `customElements.define()` called
4. **HTML parsed:** Elements upgrade automatically
5. **connectedCallback:** Component initializes for each element

For factory APIs (like `nui.components.dialog.confirm()`), the lifecycle is immediate:
- Function called
- DOM created
- Dialog shown
- Promise resolves on close
- DOM removed

## API Stability

NUI follows semantic versioning:
- Root API: Very stable
- Components namespace: Stable, additions only in minor versions
- Utilities: Stable, additions only in minor versions

Addons may have their own versioning but follow the same namespace pattern.

reference.name: architecture-patterns
reference.description: Application architecture patterns: centralized, fragment-based, and hybrid routing with nui-page layout engine.
reference.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: NUI Application Architecture ## The Layout Engine: nui-page Before diving into routing patterns, understand NUI's layout foundation: ```html
...
...
...
 ``` **Key points:** - `nui-page` wraps all page content (created by router or static) - Children are constrained to `--space-page-maxwidth` (~56rem) for readability - Add `breakout` attribute to any child to span full container width - Use `.maxwidth-container` inside breakout elements to re-constrain content The `breakout` attribute works on any element, not just `nui-layout`. ## The Router's Core Purpose NUI's router (`nui.createRouter()` / `nui.setupRouter()`) is fundamentally a **state-to-DOM coordinator**. It translates URL hash changes into DOM visibility operations. This is different from framework routers that dictate component hierarchies and data fetching. The router's job is minimal by design: 1. Parse URL hash (`#type=identifier?params`) 2. Find or create a container element for that identifier 3. Call `show()` on the active container, `hide()` on others 4. Cache containers to preserve state between navigations This simplicity enables multiple architectural patterns. ## Pattern 1: Centralized Application Logic **Philosophy:** Your application is a JavaScript program. The router is just a view controller. **How it works:** - Register feature functions with `nui.registerFeature()` - Features receive a container element and URL parameters - Features build UI programmatically using DOM APIs or templates - State management happens in your code, not the router **Best for:** - Complex applications with shared state (dashboards, editors) - Real-time collaborative apps - Apps where multiple views access the same data **The pattern in practice:** ```javascript // Centralized state const appState = { documents: [], currentDoc: null }; // Feature registration nui.registerFeature('editor', (element, params) => { // Build UI element.innerHTML = `

${appState.currentDoc?.title}

`; // Sync with state const content = element.querySelector('.editor-content'); content.textContent = appState.currentDoc?.content; // Lifecycle hooks element.show = () => { /* refresh from state */ }; }); ``` ## Pattern 2: Fragment-Based Pages **Philosophy:** Pages are HTML files. The router fetches and displays them. **How it works:** - Call `nui.enableContentLoading()` with basePath - Create HTML fragments in the pages folder - Navigation fetches fragments and injects them - Each fragment can have inline `

reference.name: declarative-actions
reference.description: Event delegation pattern using data-action attribute for CSP-safe, decoupled HTML-to-JS communication.
reference.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: Declarative Actions Pattern

## The Design Philosophy

Declarative actions in NUI solve a specific problem: **how do we connect HTML elements to JavaScript behavior without tight coupling?** The answer is event delegation with semantic syntax.

The browser already has a perfect event system - bubbling. Instead of attaching listeners to individual elements, we use a single delegated listener on a parent container. The `data-action` attribute provides the "routing" information within that container.

## Syntax as Intent

```
data-action="name:param@target"
```

This syntax encodes three pieces of information:
- **name** - What happened (the semantic action)
- **param** - Context data for the action (optional)
- **target** - Which element to operate on (optional, defaults to trigger)

This is not configuration - it's **intent declaration**. The HTML says "when clicked, the intent is to save a draft" or "the intent is to delete item #123." How that's handled is up to JavaScript.

## The Event Flow

1. **Click occurs** on an element with `data-action`
2. **NUI intercepts** at the document level
3. **Attribute is parsed** into `{ name, param, target }`
4. **CustomEvent dispatched** with these details
5. **Your code listens** and responds appropriately

The key insight: the dispatcher doesn't know what actions exist. It just parses and fires events. This decouples HTML (which declares intent) from JavaScript (which implements behavior).

## Scoping and Containment

Actions bubble like all DOM events. This enables natural component boundaries:

```javascript
// Component-level handling
card.addEventListener('nui-action', (e) => {
	e.stopPropagation(); // Keep it in the component
	handleCardAction(e.detail.name);
});

// Document-level handling (catches unscoped actions)
document.addEventListener('nui-action', (e) => {
	if (e.detail.name === 'navigate') {
		router.go(e.detail.param);
	}
});
```

This hierarchy means:
- Components can be self-contained
- Common actions (navigation) bubble up
- No explicit parent-child registration needed

## When to Use (And Not Use)

**Use declarative actions when:**
- You have lists of items with similar actions
- Elements are dynamically added/removed
- Actions are simple and contextual
- You want HTML to be readable

**Use standard event listeners when:**
- The interaction is complex (drag and drop, gestures)
- You need access to the raw event object
- The action is truly one-off

## Comparison with Framework Patterns

Unlike framework event binding (React's `onClick`, Vue's `@click`), NUI's actions don't require:
- Build step transformation
- Component re-renders
- Virtual DOM reconciliation

The browser's event system *is* the framework. NUI just adds a parsing layer and standardized event structure.

## Programmatic Registration

Sometimes you want centralized action handling without scattered listeners. `nui.registerAction()` provides this:

```javascript
nui.registerAction('save', (target, element, event, param) => {
	// Handle all 'save' actions globally
	return true; // Stop propagation
});
```

This is useful when:
- An action should behave consistently everywhere
- You want to avoid event listener setup
- Actions might come from dynamically created content

## The Simplicity Principle

The declarative actions system is intentionally minimal. It doesn't provide:
- JSON payloads in attributes (complexity in HTML)
- Action chaining (use JavaScript)
- Async action handling (listen for the event)

These limitations are features. They keep the boundary clear: HTML declares intent, JavaScript implements behavior. Neither leaks into the other's concerns.

reference.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: NUI Project Setup & Architecture ## The Essential Setup NUI requires minimal setup because it works *with* the browser, not against it. The core principle: load CSS first (to prevent FOUC), then load JavaScript as an ES module. ### Required Files **nui-theme.css** - The foundation. Defines all CSS custom properties (colors, spacing, borders) and base component styles. Must load before any NUI elements render. **nui.js** - The core library. Registers custom elements, provides the `nui` API object. Load as `type="module"` to enable ES module features. ### The Loading Sequence 1. **CSS loads** (in ``) - Elements have styling immediately 2. **HTML renders** - Native elements visible, custom elements treated as divs 3. **JS loads** - Custom elements upgrade, behaviors attach 4. **nui-ready class added** - `nui-app` signals initialization complete ### Project Structure Patterns **Standalone Page (Simplest):** Use when you need a few components on a static page. No routing, no SPA behavior. ```html ``` **SPA with Routing:** For multi-page applications. The router (`nui.setupRouter()`) intercepts hash changes and loads content fragments. Key architectural decisions: - `container: 'nui-main'` - Where fragments load - `basePath: 'pages'` - Folder containing HTML fragments - Navigation uses hash format: `#page=path/to/page` **Layout Modes:** *App Mode* (`` present): Creates a desktop-application layout with fixed header, collapsible sidebar, and scrollable content area. Uses CSS Grid with fixed positioning. *Page Mode* (no ``): Components flow in normal document flow. Good for content sites, documentation, blogs. ### The Component Registry `docs/components.json` is the single source of truth for: - Component names, categories, events - Whether a component needs addon CSS/JS - Documentation page paths Tooling (MCP tools, boilerplate generator) consumes this file. When adding components, update the registry first. ### Two Development Patterns **Declarative Pattern:** Write HTML, NUI enhances it. Best when content is known at build time. - Tabs use `
` with buttons and `
` panels - Buttons wrap native ` ``` The router parses this syntax: `action:param@target` - Single listener on container catches all clicks - `event.detail` contains `{ name, param, target }` - Dynamic content works without re-attaching listeners ### CSS Variables for Theming NUI uses a semantic color system: - `--color-base` - Background base - `--color-shade1-9` - Surface variations - `--color-highlight` - Primary accent - `--nui-space` - Base spacing unit Override in `:root` for global changes, or on specific elements for scoped theming. The `light-dark()` CSS function enables automatic dark mode support.

reference.name: introduction
reference.description: NUI philosophy: browser-native UI toolkit, semantic HTML foundation, progressive enhancement, two usage patterns.
reference.llmGuide:
# LLM Guide: NUI Philosophy & Architecture

## What NUI Is (And Isn't)

NUI is a **browser-native UI toolkit** built on Custom Elements. It is not a framework, does not have a virtual DOM, and does not abstract away the browser. Instead, it provides well-designed custom elements that enhance semantic HTML with consistent styling, accessibility behaviors, and progressive enhancement.

### Core Design Philosophy

**1. The Browser is the Framework**
- NUI uses the platform: Custom Elements, CSS Custom Properties, native `<dialog>`, `<button>`, etc.
- No virtual DOM - direct DOM manipulation is encouraged and efficient
- Event system uses native `CustomEvent` and `addEventListener`

**2. Semantic HTML as Foundation**
Every NUI component expects valid HTML inside it:
- `<nui-button>` wraps a native `<button>` element
- `<nui-dialog>` wraps a native `<dialog>` element
- `<nui-tabs>` organizes `<nav>` and `<section>` elements

This means accessibility (keyboard navigation, screen readers) works even before JavaScript loads, and the DOM remains readable and debuggable.

**3. Progressive Enhancement**
Components upgrade existing markup rather than replacing it. The page renders meaningfully without JavaScript, then components add behaviors:
- An `<nui-tabs>` with `<nav>` and `<section>` children works as plain HTML (shows all content)
- After upgrade, it becomes an interactive tab interface with ARIA attributes

**4. Two Usage Patterns**

**Declarative (HTML-first):** For static content known at authoring time
```html
<nui-button>
	<button type="submit">Save</button>
</nui-button>
```

**Data-driven (JavaScript API):** For dynamic content
```javascript
const dialog = await nui.components.dialog.confirm('Delete?', 'This cannot be undone.');
```

**5. Router as State Coordinator**
NUI includes a lightweight hash-based router (`nui.setupRouter()`) that translates URL changes into DOM visibility. It's not a framework router - it doesn't dictate your architecture. Three patterns are supported:
- **Centralized:** JavaScript generates all UI, router just shows/hides
- **Fragment-based:** HTML fragments fetched on demand (Playground pattern)
- **Hybrid:** Mix both approaches

## When to Use NUI

**Ideal for:**
- Applications that need high performance without framework overhead
- Teams that prefer direct browser API usage
- Projects where accessibility is mandatory
- SPAs that don't need complex state management

**Not ideal for:**
- Apps requiring complex reactive state management (use frameworks)
- Projects needing server-side rendering (NUI is client-side)
- Teams wanting drag-and-drop visual builders

## Key Concepts

**Light DOM, Not Shadow DOM:**
NUI components use the Light DOM. Content is visible to CSS and JavaScript without piercing shadow boundaries. This enables:
- Global theming via CSS variables
- Standard event bubbling
- Easy debugging in browser devtools

**CSS Variables for Theming:**
All styling uses CSS custom properties. The `:root` defines the design system, and components reference these variables. No CSS-in-JS, no build step required.

**State Lives in the HTML:**
Instead of hidden JavaScript state, NUI puts state in attributes and data attributes where it's visible:
- `data-tab="active"` shows which tab is active
- `hidden` attribute controls panel visibility
- This makes debugging easier and enables CSS to respond to state
